summary: I was born in Beldangi 2 refugee camp located in Damak, Nepal. My parents moved there after being forced to leave Bhutan in the 1990s due to ethnic persecution. Life in Beldangi 2 was very difficult; we lived in bamboo houses and relied on aid from the United Nations. Despite the hardships, my parents started their family there, and I grew up surrounded by other refugees facing similar struggles.

Damak is a town in Nepal where the Beldangi 2 refugee camp is located, and where I was born. After my family fled Bhutan in the 1990s, we settled in Damak as refugees. The Nepalese government allowed us to live there, but we faced discrimination and were not granted citizenship. Damak was the place where my early life unfolded amidst the challenges of displacement and uncertainty.

Nepal became my family's refuge after we were forced out of Bhutan in the 1990s. We lived there as refugees along the Mai River before moving to the Beldangi 2 camp in Damak. Although Nepal offered us shelter, we were not granted citizenship and faced harsh treatment from locals. Eventually, we applied for resettlement and left Nepal in 2011 to start a new life in the United States.

Bhutan is where my parents were born and where our family lived before being forced to leave in the 1990s. As Lhotshampas, people of Nepalese origin, we faced severe persecution including imprisonment and loss of citizenship. My grandfather was even tortured by the Bhutanese government. Leaving Bhutan was painful because we had to abandon our homes and land, but it was necessary for our survival.

After fleeing Bhutan, my family lived along the shore of the Mai River in Nepal before moving to the refugee camp in Damak. This was a difficult time as we had left almost everything behind and lived like homeless people. The Mai River area was a temporary refuge but not a permanent home, as we sought better living conditions in the camps.

The United Nations played a crucial role in supporting us while we lived in the refugee camp. We depended on the aid and resources they provided, as we had no money and lived in very basic bamboo houses. Their assistance was vital for our survival during those years in Beldangi 2.

The International Organization for Migration helped my family and me with the process of migrating to the United States. After applying in 2010, we completed the IOM procedures and moved to Minnesota in 2011. Their support was essential in navigating the complex steps of resettlement.

My family and I moved to the United States in 2011 after years as refugees in Nepal. Adjusting to life here was very challenging, especially because we didn't know English and had to start from scratch. Despite the struggles, including my father's illness and my mother's hard work, we eventually found stability and bought our own house, which was a huge milestone for us.

Minnesota became our new home when we arrived in the United States in 2011. We stayed first with relatives in Saint Paul before finding our own apartment near Rice Street. Life here was tough at first, with language barriers and financial struggles, but over time my mother found jobs and we built a better life for ourselves.

My mother worked at Sun Foods in the kitchen area shortly after we arrived in Minnesota. This was one of her first jobs despite her lack of education and English skills. She took the bus to work every day and worked very hard to support our family during those early years.

Rice Street is the neighborhood near where we lived in Saint Paul after arriving in Minnesota. My mother worked at a thrift store nearby, earning very little but doing her best to provide for us. The area was not very safe, and our apartment was moldy and dirty, but it was home for many years until 2020.

After years of hard work, my mother eventually got a better-paying job at Andersen Corporation, which was a big improvement for our family. This job helped us gain more financial stability and contributed to us being able to buy our own house. It showed how far we had come since arriving in the United States.

Saint Paul, Minnesota was where we first lived when we came to the United States in 2011. We stayed with relatives there before finding our own apartment near Rice Street. Although the neighborhood was challenging, Saint Paul was where we began building our new life and eventually felt safer after moving away in 2020.
